subject: Markets Brief — Sunday 14 June 2026
html:
📊 Markets Brief — Sunday 14 June 2026

Prepared 11:30 BST  |  For: GS Sales & Trading, London

⚠️ WEEKEND BINARY: Trump says Iran deal signs TODAY. Tehran disputes timeline (“coming days”). Oil and risk assets have largely pre-priced a deal. Monday open direction = Iran outcome.

Key Levels at a Glance

AssetLevelMove / Note
S&P 500~7,620 est.Positive week; Iran deal disinflation thesis
Nikkei 22566,078+2.90% Friday
UST 2yr4.09%—
UST 10yr4.49%2s10s +40bps; positively sloped
Bund 10yr3.05%Near 3-wk high post-ECB hike
Gilt 10yr~4.50% est.BoE event risk Thursday
EUR/USD~1.162DXY ~99.8, near 15-mth lows
GBP/USD~1.340UK CPI Wed + BoE Thu: event-dense week
USD/JPY~158BoJ intervention watch at 160
Brent$86.5/bbl↓ 4.4% Friday; ↓~20% from 2026 highs
WTI$84.9/bbl↓ 3.2% Friday
Gold spot$4,224/ozNear record territory; holding vs risk-on
Copper (LME)~$14,175/t$6.43/lb; near all-time high; supply deficit
BTC$64,385—
ETH~$1,670—

1. Overnight Market Wrap

Asian Equities (Friday close — markets closed today)

The Nikkei 225 surged +2.90% to 66,078 on Friday — one of its strongest sessions of 2026. The dual engines: Iran-deal optimism reducing energy inflation fears, and persistent USD/JPY weakness (~158) inflating yen-denominated export earnings. The Hang Seng had been volatile mid-week (-1.6% on renewed ceasefire uncertainty before recovering); the CSI 300 was last around 4,939. Both close for the weekend.

US Equities (Friday June 13)

US equities finished the week higher after a volatile fortnight. The S&P 500 is tracking near 7,600+ — it first closed above 7,600 on June 1, before the Nasdaq fell 4% on June 4 (worst day since April 2025) on a broad chip-stock repricing. The index rebounded sharply from June 10–13 as Trump and Iranian officials both signalled deal proximity, with the S&P posting +1.75% on June 11 alone. Mega-cap tech and semiconductors broadly positive into Friday’s close.

Rates

UST 2yr: 4.09% | 10yr: 4.49% | 2s10s: +40bps. The positively sloped curve is a relatively recent development — after years of inversion, normalization signals the market expects eventual Fed easing from the hold, even as the near-term is anchored by 4.2% CPI. Bund 10yr at 3.05%, near three-week highs after the ECB’s 25bps hike June 11. UK Gilts estimated in the 4.40–4.60% range; Thursday’s BoE is the next significant catalyst.

FX

DXY ~99.8, near 15-month lows. Iran-deal risk-on flows are reducing safe-haven dollar demand and simultaneously reducing the energy-inflation argument for aggressive Fed tightening. EUR/USD ~1.162 (ECB hike provides a floor; EZ stagflation caps upside to ~1.18). GBP/USD ~1.340 supported by BoE hawkishness. USD/JPY ~158 — BoJ intervention risk becomes acute above 160.

Commodities

Brent $86.5/bbl (-4.4% Friday); WTI $84.9 (-3.2%). Oil is down ~20% from 2026 highs when Iranian naval disruptions pushed prices toward $105–110. Gold spot $4,224/oz — near record territory; defying the risk-on move (see story #6). Copper ~$6.43/lb ($14,175/t), near all-time records on structural supply deficit. Mining.com snippets confirm: Glencore reversed a $300m ESG investment suspension on stronger commodity fundamentals; Ero Copper’s Furnas project in Brazil extended mineralization.

Crypto

BTC $64,385 | ETH ~$1,670. Both stabilized after BTC briefly broke $60,000 in late May/early June during peak risk-off conditions.

2. The Day Ahead — Week of 15–19 June

Time (BST)EventNote / Consensus
Sun 14 Jun (now)Iran deal signing (Trump says “today”)Tehran disputes; “coming days” likely floor
Mon 15 JunMarkets reopen; FOMC Day 1 (Tue)Monday gap direction = Iran outcome
Wed 17 Jun, 07:00UK CPI — May 2026 (ONS)Key BoE input; prior May reading elevated
Wed 17 Jun, 19:00FOMC Rate Decision99.5% hold at 3.5–3.75%; dot plot & SEP
Wed 17 Jun, ~19:30Fed Chair Warsh press conferenceBias shift? Hike-readiness language? THE event
Thu 18 Jun, 12:00Bank of England MPC DecisionConsensus hold 3.75%; prior vote 8-1 for hold

Also watch: US retail sales and housing data likely mid-week — confirm via live calendar Monday.

3. What’s Moving Markets

1 The Iran Deal — THE Weekend Binary

Trump wrote on Truth Social that the deal is “scheduled to get signed tomorrow,” (June 14) with the Strait of Hormuz reopening immediately. Confirmed deal terms include: Strait reopening; dismantlement of Iran’s nuclear enrichment programme; removal and destruction of enriched uranium stockpile; international inspections; phased sanction relief. Tehran’s Foreign Ministry publicly disputes the Sunday timeline (“will not be tomorrow”) but has not denied the deal is close. UK PM Starmer called Trump Saturday to express support; Pakistan PM Sharif said finalization was “likely in the next 24 hours.” The market has pre-priced the deal substantially. On signing: incremental oil move may be limited (-$3 to -$5), equities gap modestly higher. On failure: oil +$5–10, risk-off, violent squeeze of spec shorts.

2 Oil — Priced for Peace, Not Yet for Policy

Even with a deal, physical oil supply normalization takes months. Iranian capacity is ~3–3.5m b/d but has been suppressed; restoring even 1m b/d of net supply is a multi-month process requiring sanction unwinding and US Congressional involvement. OPEC+ is the critical wildcard — Saudi Arabia’s fiscal breakeven is ~$90/bbl and the Kingdom has consistently managed the price floor. A sustained break below $80 Brent would likely trigger an OPEC+ production cut response. Goldman Sachs had estimated the Iran war shaved ~0.3% off global GDP; the reversal is a meaningful macro tailwind, but the oil market should be seen as moving in a range of $75–90 over the next quarter, not collapsing to $60.

3 FOMC Wednesday — Warsh’s First Dot Plot

The June 16–17 FOMC is Kevin Warsh’s first meeting with a full dot plot and SEP. Rate hold at 3.5–3.75% is essentially certain (99.5% market-implied probability). Three things matter: (a) does the median 2026 dot shift back toward 4%, reintroducing the hike scenario? (b) does Warsh formally abandon Powell’s easing bias? (c) how does the SEP handle the energy/inflation interaction — does falling oil reduce the inflation forecast enough to bring the dot plot lower, or does sticky core keep it elevated? US CPI at 4.2% YoY, 220bps above target, gives Warsh every justification for a hawkish press conference tone. The 2yr UST at 4.09% would need to re-rate toward 4.25–4.40% if hike language reappears in the dot plot.

4 ECB’s Stagflation Hike

The ECB raised its deposit rate 25bps to 2.25% on June 11 — the first hike since 2023. Rationale: eurozone headline CPI hit 3.2% in May (core 2.5%), driven by Iran-war energy pass-through. The backdrop is stagflationary: EZ GDP contracted -0.2% in Q1 2026; the ECB now forecasts 2026 growth of just 0.8%. Lagarde framed the hike as “pre-emptive against entrenched inflation” while acknowledging deteriorating growth. The Bund at 3.05% is near recent highs. A second ECB hike is on the table for Q3 — but Iran-deal driven oil disinflation could make it unnecessary, and the ECB will be deeply reluctant to further damage already-fragile EZ economies. The EUR/USD ceiling is ~1.18 in this stagflation context.

5 Bank of England — Knife-Edge Vote

The April MPC vote was 8-1 — one Hawk voting for an immediate 25bps hike to 4.00%. This was the most hawkish split since early 2023. UK services CPI has been sticky above 5%, and the Iran-war energy pass-through added to headline inflation. Wednesday’s UK CPI print is critical: if May CPI comes in above 3.5% headline or services CPI accelerates, a second hawk may emerge for Thursday’s decision. The base case remains an 8-1 hold at 3.75%, but the risk is a 7-2 or even 6-3 vote that would reprice GBP sharply higher. Cable at 1.340 has some hawkish BoE already priced; 7-2 vote could push it toward 1.355–1.365.

6 Gold Defying the Risk-On Narrative at $4,224

Gold spot at $4,224/oz is striking — geopolitical risk is fading with the Iran deal, yet gold holds near record territory. The explanation is structural: (1) central bank buying from China, India, Turkey and EM sovereigns running persistent diversification programmes away from dollar reserves; (2) DXY at ~99.8 (15-month lows) provides a direct tailwind; (3) real US rates are not rising — the Fed is on hold and 4.2% CPI keeps real rates depressed. The one clean catalyst for a significant gold sell-off would be a Warsh press conference that aggressively signals rate hike readiness, spiking real rates and strengthening the dollar. Without that, gold looks supported.

7 Copper Near $14,000/t — Structural Deficit

LME copper at ~$14,175/t ($6.43/lb), near all-time records. The Goldman Sachs-estimated 330,000 tonne supply deficit in 2026 is structural, not cyclical. AI data centres require ~12x more copper per MW than conventional builds; EV batteries and grid upgrades add to demand. Chilean state copper commission Cochilco raised its 2026 forecast to ~$5.55/lb. Chinese smelters are buying pyrite as a concentrate substitute — a sign of genuine raw-material tightness. Mining companies are benefiting: Glencore reversed a $300m ESG investment suspension; Ero Copper extended mineralization at Furnas, Brazil. This structural story is not derailed by an Iran deal.

8 Japan — Nikkei 66,000, BoJ Watching 160

The Nikkei’s +2.90% Friday session to 66,078 reflects the dual tailwinds of weak yen and global AI/tech enthusiasm. USD/JPY at ~158 inflates yen-based export earnings for Toyota, Sony, Shin-Etsu, and the semiconductor equipment complex (Advantest, Tokyo Electron). The BoJ faces a classic dilemma: raise rates and risk a brutal carry-trade unwind as the most crowded FX position in markets reverses; stay on hold and risk yen weakness toward 160 where Ministry of Finance intervention previously triggered. The BoJ is likely to jawbone aggressively if USD/JPY tests 160 this week.

4. Desk Angle

The Weekend Binary. The single most important call for a desk opening Monday is the Iran deal status. Oil and risk assets have substantially pre-priced the deal (oil -20%, equities rallied, DXY soft). This means:

  • Deal signs: incremental move in oil limited (-$3–5 more); equities gap modestly higher; focus shifts to implementation timeline. Sell-the-news is a genuine risk in energy.
  • Deal doesn’t print: oil snaps back $5–10 toward $92+; equities gap lower; spec short squeeze in energy. Violent but potentially short-lived.

FOMC Vol Positioning. Wednesday’s FOMC is generating rates volatility interest despite the near-certain hold. SOFR options are elevated for a no-change meeting, suggesting real positioning around a Warsh hawkish surprise. The consensus “Warsh trade” is short 2yr UST or short SOFR Dec-2026.

GBP Event Stack. UK CPI Wednesday + BoE Thursday makes sterling the most event-dense currency of the week. CFTC data shows GBP longs building. An upside CPI surprise + hawkish BoE vote shift (to 7-2 or 6-3) could move cable 1.0–1.5 big figures quickly.

Sector Flows: Energy majors (BP, Shell, XOM) likely to face continued selling on Iran-deal oil headwind. Miners (Antofagasta, Freeport, Glencore) remain structural buy-on-dip. Defensives underperforming in current risk-on; watch utilities and consumer staples for outflows.

Risk Skew: Oil downside appears limited to $75–80 (OPEC+ will cut below that); upside to $92–95 if deal fails. Asymmetric from current levels — limited downside, meaningful upside risk. Rates: 2yr UST hike premia understated vs Warsh’s track record. Gold: Warsh hawkish shock is the only clean directional catalyst for a significant move lower; otherwise structurally supported.

5. Story Follow-Ups

This is the inaugural brief. Story tracker initialized today with 7 active stories. Follow-up sections will appear from the next brief forward.

6. Trade Ideas

Illustrative only — not investment advice. For desk discussion only.

1. Sell Brent on Iran-Deal Confirmation — Target $80, Stop $91
If Strait deal signs early this week, sell August Brent near $86–87. Much is priced but $85 break opens $80 over 4–6 weeks as Iranian supply restoration begins. Instrument: August Brent futures or Brent put spread $85/$77. Key risk: deal fails, oil reverses to $92+.
2. 2s10s Steepener into FOMC (Rates)
Short 2yr UST / Long 10yr UST: if Warsh dot plot signals potential 2026 hikes, the 2yr re-rates toward 4.25%; simultaneously falling oil lowers long-run inflation expectations, anchoring the 10yr or pulling it lower. 2s10s steepener from +40bps toward +55–60bps. Instrument: DV01-matched UST or SOFR swap. Key risk: Warsh turns surprisingly dovish.
3. Long Copper (LME 3-Month) on Dips to $13,500/t
Structural 330,000t supply deficit + AI infrastructure demand = multi-year bull story. Any macro-driven pullback is a buying opportunity. Instrument: LME 3-month futures; alternatively Antofagasta (ANTO LN) or Freeport-McMoRan (FCX US). Key risk: China hard-landing crushing industrial demand.
4. Long GBP/USD Near 1.335 — UK Event-Week Upside
UK CPI (Wed) + BoE (Thu) = event-dense week. If CPI surprises high and MPC vote shifts to 7-2 or 6-3 for a hike, cable could move from 1.340 toward 1.355–1.365. Entry on intraday dip to 1.335; stop at 1.320. Key risk: UK CPI undershoots, BoE stays 8-1 and flags growth headwinds.
Sources checked:
✅ Web search (Google) — general markets data
✅ NBC News, ABC News, CBS News, Al Jazeera — Iran deal coverage (snippets)
✅ FRED St. Louis Fed — Treasury yield data
✅ Polymarket / prediction markets — FOMC probability data
✅ Mining.com (search snippets only — site returned 403)
⚠️ CNBC.com, TheStreet, Seeking Alpha, Bloomberg, FT — 403 Forbidden / paywalled; data via Google snippets only
⚠️ Aurelian Research Substack (aurelionresearch.substack.com) — 403 Forbidden; skipped
⚠️ Macro Trading Floor podcast — Spotify not web-accessible; no YouTube episode found; skipped
⚠️ Trading Economics (tradingeconomics.com) — 403 Forbidden; data via snippets
❌ Gmail inbox — not connected in this session; skipped

Data note: Several specific closing levels (e.g., S&P 500 Friday June 13 exact close) are estimates based on directional context; direct financial data sites blocked paywalls. Levels accurate as of June 12–13 data retrieved via search.
